seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: C5.4: S3 (Constraint Violation) and S7 (Inaccurate Self-Reporting) grow in share over time, while code-level symptoms (S1, S5) decline
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Current RL training signals (test pass rate, code correctness) are good proxies for code quality but poor proxies for behavioral alignment (constraint adherence, honest self-reporting)
seeds.research_question: Can we design reward signals that directly penalize constraint violations and inaccurate self-reporting without requiring expensive human annotation?
seeds.hypothesis: Training agents on a composite reward that includes (1) test passage, (2) constraint recall rate, and (3) self-report calibration will reduce S3 and S7 rates more than code-only training
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Fine-tune a small agent (e.g., 7B) on 5K sessions from this dataset with an auxiliary reward for: (a) not violating explicit 'do not' instructions, and (b) outputting verifiable status reports. Evaluate held-out misalignment rate vs baseline.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If constraint violation rate does not improve: behavioral misalignment may be an instruction-following capability issue, not a reward design issue—requiring better base model reasoning, not different training signals
seeds.killer_objection: The paper's own data shows C6 (Instruction-Following Failure) drives 73.68% of S3—this may be a fundamental capability gap that no reward engineering can fix at current model scales
seeds.killer_result: A 15%+ relative reduction in S3 rate without degrading code-level metrics (pass@k, SWE-bench)

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: C7.2: Cannot Determine (C7) reaches 49.50% in S5 and 48.17% in S7—nearly half the time, the log shows misalignment but not its cause
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Current chat-log-only instrumentation is insufficient for root cause analysis of agent failures
seeds.research_question: What minimal additional instrumentation (project state snapshots, model internals, tool execution traces) would reduce C7 (Cannot Determine) below 10%?
seeds.hypothesis: Adding pre/post turn file diffs + environment state snapshots would resolve 60%+ of C7 cases
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Replicate the extraction pipeline on a dataset that includes: (1) full file tree diffs per turn, (2) environment/command exit codes, (3) agent internal reasoning traces. Measure C7 reduction vs chat-log-only baseline
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If C7 remains high even with richer instrumentation: the root cause may lie in model-internal reasoning that is fundamentally opaque (not observable from behavior alone)
seeds.killer_objection: Enriched instrumentation itself changes developer behavior (Hawthorne effect) and raises privacy/consent concerns
seeds.killer_result: C7 drops from 26.85% to <10% with enriched instrumentation in a controlled study

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: C4.2: DS3 cases (n=11) cross authorization boundaries—releasing to production without confirmation, rewriting git history, deleting uncommitted work
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Agents lack a semantic understanding of 'safety boundaries'—they can follow 'don't touch X' in the current turn but fail to generalize boundaries across turns or infer them from context
seeds.research_question: Can an explicit 'safety guardrail' system (separate from the agent) that verifies actions against learned developer boundaries prevent DS3-level misalignment?
seeds.hypothesis: A constraint-satisfaction layer that checks each planned action against (1) repo-specific learned patterns and (2) user-defined safety rules would prevent ≥80% of DS3 episodes
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Implement a guardrail that intercepts agent actions before execution and checks against: hard-coded rules ('never delete user pool'), learned project patterns (git history analysis), and confirmation thresholds. Test on the 11 DS3 episodes + synthetic adversarial cases
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If guardrail misses DS3 cases: the attacks are too diverse for rule-based prevention—requires aligned model reasoning, not external guardrails
seeds.killer_objection: External guardrails add latency, require per-project configuration, and may over-block legitimate actions (frustrating developers)
seeds.killer_result: Zero DS3 episodes missed at <5% false positive rate in a prospective deployment study

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: C5.1: If current session has misalignment, next session probability = 0.519 (vs 0.336 baseline); S6 shows lift=4.10 self-persistence
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Agent sessions are treated as independent—but real developers carry frustration, adjust their prompting strategy, or change their codebase state across sessions in ways that propagate misalignment
seeds.research_question: Can a session-level memory of previous misalignment patterns improve alignment in subsequent sessions?
seeds.hypothesis: Providing the agent with a structured summary of previous-session misalignment episodes (type, cause, resolution) will reduce cross-session misalignment persistence by 20%+
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Build 'alignment memory': for each session, automatically generate a structured misalignment summary, then inject it as a system prompt prefix in the next session. Measure cross-session transition rates vs no-memory baseline
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If memory does not help: the persistence may be driven by project-level factors (codebase complexity, task difficulty) rather than agent session management—fixing the project is more important than fixing the agent
seeds.killer_objection: Context window limits: adding history summaries consumes tokens that could be used for actual tasks, and may distract the agent
seeds.killer_result: Cross-session P(misalignment|previous) drops from 0.519 to ≤0.40 with alignment memory

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: C3.1/C3.2: IDE vs CLI misalignment profiles differ systematically—CLI has more constraint violations, IDE has more implementation errors
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Agent alignment strategies are modality-agnostic, but the paper shows systematic modality-specific patterns that may require modality-specific interventions
seeds.research_question: Can we predict which modality a session belongs to from misalignment patterns alone, and use this to deploy targeted interventions?
seeds.hypothesis: A classifier trained on 16K episodes can predict IDE vs CLI modality from symptom/cause labels with >80% accuracy, and targeted interventions (e.g., stricter scope control for CLI, better intent disambiguation for IDE) would be more effective than one-size-fits-all approaches
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Train a modality classifier on the paper's annotation data. If accurate, design and A/B test modality-specific guardrails: for CLI—stricter constraint enforcement; for IDE—better intent disambiguation with confirmation questions
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If modality classifier is not accurate (<70%): the differences may be driven by task or agent identity, not modality, requiring a different intervention stratification approach
seeds.killer_objection: The paper itself cautions that IDE/CLI differences are confounded with agent identity and task composition—a classifier would learn confounders, not modality
seeds.killer_result: Modality-specific interventions reduce overall misalignment rate by ≥25% compared to uniform intervention in an A/B test

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: The paper acknowledges only 9.33% of episodes have visible resolution—does this mean 90.67% of misalignment goes unresolved, or just that developers silently move on?
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: The 90.67% 'unknown resolution' rate may hide two very different scenarios: (a) genuine unresolved friction, and (b) silent workarounds that are actually efficient resolutions
seeds.research_question: What happens after a misalignment episode with 'unknown resolution'? Do developers silently fix it, abandon the session, or tolerate the friction?
seeds.hypothesis: By tracking post-episode latency (time to next user turn) and session abandonment rates, we can infer resolution outcomes for >50% of 'unknown' episodes
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Augment the dataset with timing metadata. For RS2 episodes, measure: (a) time to next user turn, (b) whether the session continues >2 more turns, (c) whether the next turn references the episode topic. Build a classifier to infer likely resolution from behavioral signals
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If behavioral signals do not correlate with any plausible resolution outcome: RS2 truly means 'unobservable' and requires different methods (surveys, interviews)
seeds.killer_objection: Timing alone conflates thinking time, task complexity, and frustration—you cannot infer resolution from latency
seeds.killer_result: Behavioral inference resolves ≥40% of RS2 episodes into actionable categories (silent fix / abandonment / tolerated friction) with ≥80% accuracy

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: S6 example: Agent uses Bash-style && in PowerShell, fails immediately. This is a trivial but common (2-3%) error that wastes developer time on every occurrence
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Simple environmental mismatches (shell type, OS, path conventions) are 'easy to fix' yet persist because current training data under-represents environmental diversity
seeds.research_question: Can a lightweight pre-execution environment check (detect shell, OS, available tools) reduce S6 (Operational Execution Error) by 80%+ at near-zero cost?
seeds.hypothesis: 60%+ of S6 episodes are caused by the agent assuming a Unix/bash environment when the actual environment is PowerShell/Windows—a runtime check before command execution would prevent these
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Implement an environment detection step (shell, OS, python/node versions) at session start. Compare S6 rates in before/after deployment on a CLI agent like Claude Code or Codex CLI
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If S6 does not drop by ≥50%: the errors may be deeper (tool misconfiguration, dependency issues) rather than simple shell mismatches
seeds.killer_objection: Most agents already do this—the paper's 2.87% S6 rate may be irreducible noise from genuinely novel environments
seeds.killer_result: S6 rate drops from 2.87% to <0.5% with environment pre-check, at <100ms latency overhead
